title: Hiring Your Children in Family Business

requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must legitimately employ children in family business with reasonable compensation for actual work performed to shift income to children's lower tax brackets while business deducts wages and avoids certain payroll taxes
criteria: Child must perform legitimate work appropriate for age and actually perform the work claimed
Compensation must be reasonable for work performed and comparable to what would be paid to non-family member
Maintain proper documentation: time records, job descriptions, pay stubs, and work product evidence
Children under 18 working in parent's sole proprietorship or partnership (only parents) exempt from FICA taxes
Children under 21 exempt from FUTA tax when working in parent's sole proprietorship or partnership
Must file W-2 and follow proper payroll procedures even if FICA exempt
Child's wages deductible by business; child files own return using standard deduction ($14,600 for 2024)
Consider Roth IRA contributions for child using earned income (up to $7,000 or earned income, whichever less)

employing_children_rules.business_deduction: Business deducts wages as ordinary and necessary business expense
employing_children_rules.fica_exemption: Children under 18 working for parent's sole prop/partnership (parents only) exempt from FICA
employing_children_rules.futa_exemption: Children under 21 exempt from FUTA tax in parent's sole prop/partnership
employing_children_rules.child_standard_deduction: Child uses standard deduction ($14,600 for 2024, $15,000 for 2025) sheltering wages from tax
employing_children_rules.income_shifting: Shifts income from parent's high bracket (potentially 37%) to child's low/zero bracket
employing_children_rules.roth_ira_opportunity: Child can contribute to Roth IRA using earned income for long-term tax-free growth

restrictions: S Corporations and C Corporations do not qualify for FICA exemption; must pay payroll taxes on children's wages
IRS scrutinizes legitimacy of work, reasonableness of compensation, and actual performance
Child must actually perform work; paper-only arrangements disallowed and subject to penalties
Excessive compensation relative to work performed may be disallowed or reclassified as gift
Must maintain contemporaneous time records and documentation showing work performed
Children subject to income tax on wages exceeding standard deduction amount
Unearned income (kiddie tax) rules don't apply to earned wages but affect investment income
State child labor laws may restrict hours, times, and types of work children can perform
